content.text: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked APIs around resources, standard HTTP methods, and stateless interactions. Agent platforms expose runs, sessions, tools, and memories as HTTP resources; tool integrations consume third-party REST APIs — understanding REST principles keeps interfaces predictable, cacheable, and easy for both humans and autonomous agents to consume correctly.

is an architectural style, not a protocol or standard. Roy Fielding defined it in his 2000 doctoral dissertation as a set of constraints for building scalable, loosely-coupled distributed systems over HTTP. A REST API models its domain as
— named objects identified by URLs — and exposes a uniform set of HTTP
(verbs) to read, create, modify, and delete those objects. Responses carry
of the resource state, usually JSON.
— UI and API evolve independently; the boundary is the HTTP contract.
— every request carries all context (auth, parameters); the server holds no per-client session state.
— responses declare whether they can be cached; GET responses on unchanged resources yield 304 Not Modified.
— consistent resource identification, manipulation via representations, self-descriptive messages, and HATEOAS.
— clients cannot tell whether they are talking directly to the origin server or through a proxy/cache layer.
— server may send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to extend client behavior.
Practical subset used in agent APIs
— GET reads, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH updates, DELETE removes

Agent frameworks like LangChain, AutoGen, and custom orchestrators generate HTTP calls automatically from tool definitions. When an API follows REST conventions, the LLM can infer the right URL and method from the resource name alone:
obviously creates an agent run. Deviating from conventions (everything as
RPC) still works but forces extra tool descriptions, breaks caching proxies, makes retry logic ambiguous, and prevents clients from using auto-generated SDK code.

REST also maps cleanly to OpenAPI/Swagger specifications, which LLM tool-calling infrastructure (function schemas, MCP tools) can consume directly. A well-designed REST API becomes a zero-shot callable tool for an agent without hand-written wrappers.
— any HTTP client (curl, requests, fetch, axios) works without a vendor SDK
— GET responses can be served from CDN or proxy cache, reducing latency for repeated reads
— hierarchical URL structure reflects the domain model and self-documents the API shape
— stateless requests can be routed to any backend instance without sticky sessions
— OpenAPI, Postman, httpx, and code generators all consume standard REST patterns
— function-call schemas map cleanly to REST endpoints; names are semantically rich
) allow breaking changes without forcing immediate client migration
— a single GET returns the whole resource even when the caller needs one field; multiple GETs needed to assemble related data
are hard to evolve without breaking clients
— hypermedia links in responses are theoretically powerful but uncommon in real APIs
— complex commands (batch operations, multi-step workflows) fit awkwardly into resource CRUD
— per-request TLS handshake and header overhead versus gRPC's binary framing or WebSocket streams
is not strictly RESTful; the response is not a persistent resource

# Resource collections (plural nouns)
GET    /v1/runs                  # list all runs
POST   /v1/runs                  # create a new run

# Specific resource instance
GET    /v1/runs/{run_id}          # read a single run
PATCH  /v1/runs/{run_id}          # partial update (e.g., cancel)
DELETE /v1/runs/{run_id}          # delete a run

# Sub-resources (nested under parent)
GET    /v1/runs/{run_id}/messages  # messages belonging to run
POST   /v1/runs/{run_id}/messages  # add message to run

# Action sub-resources (RPC-style when needed)
POST   /v1/runs/{run_id}/cancel    # cancel action on a run
POST   /v1/tools/{tool_id}/invoke  # invoke tool (acceptable pattern)
Status codes are the primary error-communication channel. Clients branch on the numeric class, then read the body for detail.
GET, PUT, PATCH that return data
POST that persists a new resource
DELETE or action with no return value
Every request must include all context needed to fulfill it: authentication (
header or path parameter), and correlation IDs (
). The server must never rely on previously stored session state between requests. This lets any backend instance service any request — fundamental to horizontal scaling.
Clients signal the desired format with
triggers SSE. Versioning via URL prefix (
) is simpler and more visible than header-based versioning (
) — choose one strategy and document it in your OpenAPI spec.

The diagram shows the core layers: a client or agent sends HTTP requests with authentication credentials to the REST API layer, which routes requests to resource handlers. Each resource maps to a URL path and responds with JSON representations. The underlying persistence layer (database, cache) holds actual state; the API layer never stores session state between calls.
— expose runs, sessions, tools, memories as resources with stable versioned URLs
— when the external service (Slack, GitHub, Stripe) already exposes a REST API the agent calls via HTTP
— documents, users, configurations, audit logs — data that maps naturally to create/read/update/delete
— read-heavy endpoints that benefit from CDN or reverse-proxy caching (GET is cache-safe by default)
— for high-throughput internal service-to-service calls where binary framing reduces latency
— when callers have widely different field needs and over-fetching is a real cost
— for actions with no natural resource shape (
The following example builds a minimal agent-run REST API with FastAPI. It demonstrates resource naming, correct status codes, idempotent cancellation, and a Location header on create.
The following snippet shows a Python client that uses the httpx library to call the REST API above. It creates a run, polls for completion, and retrieves messages — a common agent polling pattern.

import httpx, time

BASE = "http://localhost:8000"
HEADERS = {"Authorization": "Bearer test-token-123",
           "Content-Type": "application/json"}


def create_and_poll(model: str, system: str, user_msg: str) -> dict:
    # 1. Create a run
    r = httpx.post(f"{BASE}/v1/runs",
                   json={"model": model,
                         "system_prompt": system,
                         "user_message": user_msg},
                   headers=HEADERS)
    r.raise_for_status()
    run = r.json()
    run_id = run["id"]
    print(f"Created run {run_id}, status={run['status']}")

    # 2. Poll until terminal state
    terminal = {"completed", "failed", "cancelled"}
    for attempt in range(60):
        time.sleep(2)
        r = httpx.get(f"{BASE}/v1/runs/{run_id}", headers=HEADERS)
        r.raise_for_status()
        run = r.json()
        print(f"  [{attempt+1}] status={run['status']}")
        if run["status"] in terminal:
            break
    else:
        print("Timeout — cancelling run")
        httpx.post(f"{BASE}/v1/runs/{run_id}/cancel", headers=HEADERS)

    return run


def list_completed_runs() -> list[dict]:
    r = httpx.get(f"{BASE}/v1/runs?status=completed", headers=HEADERS)
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = create_and_poll(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        system="You are a helpful assistant.",
        user_msg="Summarise the REST principles in three sentences."
    )
    print("Final run status:", result["status"])
    completed = list_completed_runs()
    print(f"{len(completed)} completed run(s) on record")

The Anthropic Messages API follows REST conventions: POST /v1/messages creates a new conversation turn (the message is the resource); GET /v1/models lists available models. The OpenAI Assistants API is more fully RESTful — POST /v1/threads creates a thread, POST /v1/threads/{id}/runs starts an agent run, and GET /v1/threads/{id}/runs/{run_id} polls for completion. Clients poll until status reaches 'completed' or 'failed', then call GET /v1/threads/{id}/messages to retrieve the assistant's reply.

GitHub's REST API is a canonical public example: GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues lists issues, POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues creates one, PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number} closes it (status: 'closed'). Every LLM agent that interacts with GitHub calls these endpoints — following the same pattern in your own APIs makes them equally agent-accessible.
— GET must be safe and idempotent; crawlers, CDNs, and prefetch logic will trigger it unintentionally, causing data corruption
in the body forces clients to parse every body to detect failure; HTTP status codes are the standard contract
duplicates information already conveyed by GET/DELETE; use nouns
expose total record counts and invite enumeration attacks; prefer opaque UUIDs or ULID
so clients can retrieve the new resource without re-parsing the body
means retries on network failure corrupt data; use a separate resource or idempotency keys
across endpoints forces agent tools to write format-specific parsers; standardise on RFC 9457 Problem Details
— agents retry on network failures; without an idempotency key header the same run is created twice
Use plural nouns for collections and singular path parameters for instances
Return 201 + Location header on create; 204 (no body) on delete
Use 404 for absent resources, 409 for state conflicts, 422 for schema validation
Prefix all paths with a version segment:
Support idempotency keys (Idempotency-Key header) on POST for safe agent retries
Return consistent error bodies with at least code (machine-readable) and message (human-readable)
Document your API with OpenAPI 3.x — it becomes an auto-importable tool schema for LLM agents
Limit nesting depth to two levels (
Use snake_case for JSON field names consistently
Paginate list endpoints from day one — even small lists grow

REST's stateless constraint is the primary lever for horizontal scalability. Because no server-side session state ties a client to a specific instance, any node behind a load balancer can handle any request. This lets you add capacity by simply launching more instances.
to list endpoints that tolerate slight staleness; use
for conditional GETs that save bandwidth on unchanged resources
) scales better than offset/limit for large tables; return
— HTTP/1.1 keep-alive and HTTP/2 multiplexing eliminate per-request TLS handshake overhead when agents call the API repeatedly in a loop
handlers release the event-loop thread during DB I/O, allowing thousands of concurrent long-poll requests on a single process
for large JSON bodies; tool-call responses with embedded text chunks benefit significantly
so agent loops can back off gracefully before hitting 429
Good for simple APIs; add-on provides Swagger UI
Preferred for agents; sync + async APIs
Machine-readable contract; imports as LLM tool schema
Auto-generated from OpenAPI; test endpoints in browser
FastAPI's default; models become OpenAPI schemas
Test REST endpoints with ASGI test client
Cleaner rendering of OpenAPI spec than Swagger UI

Use REST as the default for any public API an agent will call. Prefer gRPC for internal high-frequency service calls where binary framing and generated stubs reduce overhead. Consider GraphQL only when front-end clients with diverse field requirements are the primary consumers.
header; validate signature, expiry, and audience on every request
in environment variables, never in source code
— grant minimum necessary access; an agent tool calling
Validate all path parameters and query strings server-side even if the client is also validated
Reject oversized bodies early (Content-Length check) to prevent memory exhaustion
Sanitize string fields used in database queries — use parameterized queries, never string concatenation
on POST; return the stored response if the same key is retried within a TTL window
— include on 429 and 503 responses so agent loops know the minimum delay before retrying
— when downstream services fail, return 503 fast rather than hanging; agents time out otherwise
through every layer; echo it in error responses so agent logs and API logs can be correlated
Enforce HTTPS for all production endpoints; redirect HTTP to HTTPS
Set HSTS header to prevent protocol downgrade attacks
Restrict CORS origins to known clients; do not use wildcard (*) in production

Think of a REST API as a filing cabinet. Each drawer (URL path) holds a specific type of document (resource). You open a drawer with GET, add a file with POST, replace a file with PUT, update a page with PATCH, and shred a file with DELETE. The filing system works regardless of who you are (stateless) — you just need to show your ID (Authorization header) with every visit.

Agents navigate a REST API the same way: they follow the URL hierarchy to locate the right resource, apply the semantically correct HTTP method, and interpret the numeric status code to decide whether to retry, fail fast, or proceed. A well-named REST API is almost self-documenting to an LLM.
Model domain data as resources identified by URLs; keep paths as nouns, use HTTP methods as verbs
Every request must be stateless — auth and context travel in headers, not server-side sessions
Use correct HTTP status codes as the primary error-communication channel
Return 201 + Location on create, 204 on delete, 404 on missing resource, 409 on state conflict
Version all public APIs from day one with a URL prefix such as /v1/
Support idempotency keys on POST endpoints so agents can safely retry on network failure
Generate an OpenAPI spec — it becomes a zero-boilerplate importable tool schema for LLM agents
REST is the universal default for public APIs; use gRPC for internal high-throughput calls
